statement.markdown: Solve the problem and fix the bugs.
statement.outputFormat: int the value dequeued at index k from a circular queue of size n

hints.text: The issue is with the index calculation. Remember that the queue is 0-indexed.

editorial.title: Invalid Dequeue from Circular Queue
editorial.optimalSolution: A circular queue is a FIFO (First-In-First-Out) data structure that uses a single array to store elements. To dequeue an element, we need to check the current position (i.e., the head) and the size of the queue. If the size of the queue is greater than or equal to the current position, we dequeue the element at the current position. Otherwise, we need to move the head to the previous position.

buggyStarterCode.cpp: class Solution { public: int deque(int n, int k) { return (n + k) % (n - 1); } };
buggyStarterCode.java: public class Solution { public int deque(int n, int k) { return (n + k) % (n - 1); } }
buggyStarterCode.python: class Solution: def deque(self, n: int, k: int) -> int: return (n + k) % (n - 1);
buggyStarterCode.javascript: var Solution = function() { return (function(n, k) { return (n + k) % (n - 1); }); };

functionSignature.python: int deque(self, n: int, k: int) -> int;

solutions.python:
import sys

class Solution:
    def __init__(self):
        self.nums = []

    def solve(self):
        n, k = map(int, input().split())
        self.nums.append(n)
        self.nums.append(k)
        self.nums.append(0)

        for i in range(self.nums[0]):
            self.nums.append(i)

        result = self.nums[self.nums[1]+1]

        return result

solve = Solution()
solve.solve()
